title: CDC STEADI Protocol: A Complete Implementation Guide
description: CDC STEADI protocol implementation guide. Learn the 3-step algorithm, how to screen, assess and intervene for falls risk in older adults.

blocks.question: What is the CDC STEADI Protocol and how do I implement it?
blocks.directAnswer: STEADI is the CDC's evidence-based 3-step algorithm for falls prevention in adults aged 65+. It combines the 3 Key Questions screening tool with functional assessments like the Timed Up and Go test. Implementation takes 5-15 minutes per patient and requires minimal staff training, making it ideal for clinical and community settings.

tldrBullets.text: 1 in 4 adults aged 65+ falls annually, costing $50 billion in direct medical care (CDC, 2015)
STEADI reduces fall risk through structured screening, assessment and intervention
Non-clinical staff can administer the 4 core functional tests with minimal training

blocks.expandedAnswer:
The CDC STEADI (Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths, and Injuries) protocol is a three-step algorithm designed specifically for primary care and community settings. It begins with screening using the 3 Key Questions to identify fall risk, moves to assessment with validated functional tests, and concludes with targeted interventions based on results.

Falls are the leading cause of both unintentional injury deaths and non-fatal trauma in older adults. The economic burden is substantial: direct medical costs reached $50 billion in the United States in 2015, with projections suggesting $101 billion by 2030 if prevention efforts do not scale. STEADI addresses this crisis through a systematic, evidence-based framework that integrates seamlessly into routine care workflows.

What makes STEADI particularly effective is its scalability. The screening phase takes 2-3 minutes. The assessment phase, using Able Assess or manual tests, takes 5-15 minutes total. Staff training requirements are minimal, typically under 15 minutes for basic competency. This accessibility means STEADI can be deployed rapidly across primary care clinics, community health centers, retirement communities, and care facilities without significant infrastructure investment.

sources.title: CDC STEADI: Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths, and Injuries
The Epidemiology and Impact of Falls in Older Adults
National Healthcare Quality and Disparities Report (NHQDR)

blocks.body:
Step 1: Screen for Fall Risk Using the 3 Key Questions

The screening phase is brief and effective. Ask all patients aged 65+ these three questions: "Have you fallen in the past year?", "Do you feel unsteady when standing or walking?", and "Do you worry about falling?" If the patient answers yes to any question, they progress to the assessment phase. If all answers are no, document the result and reassess annually.

The 3 Key Questions have strong sensitivity and specificity for identifying fall risk. A study in the American Journal of Preventive Medicine found that 2 or more yes responses predict future falls with 88% sensitivity. This efficiency means you can screen large cohorts quickly, reserving assessment time for those who need it most. Learn more about STEADI screening.

Document screening results in the patient record. This establishes a baseline and enables tracking of changes over time. If your organization uses Able Assess, the screening data syncs automatically, creating a complete digital record that supports continuity of care.

Step 2: Assess Functional Capacity With 4 Core Tests

Patients who screen positive progress to functional assessment. The four core tests measure different aspects of mobility and balance: grip strength testing (using a hand dynamometer), the 30-Second Chair Stand test (how many times a person can stand from a chair in 30 seconds), the Timed Up and Go test (time to rise from a chair, walk 3 meters, and return), and the 4-Stage Balance Test (progressively challenging standing positions).

These tests are validated across hundreds of studies and predict falls, hospitalization risk, and disability progression in older adults. A meta-analysis in the Cochrane Database found that multi-component assessment programs using these tests reduce fall incidence by 17-24% when coupled with appropriate interventions. The tests require no special equipment beyond a chair and stopwatch, though hand dynamometers improve precision for grip testing.

Non-clinical staff can administer these tests reliably after brief training. A 2022 systematic review in the Journal of the American Geriatrics Society demonstrated that trained health workers, care coordinators, and community health workers achieve equivalent measurement reliability to clinical physiotherapists. This democratization of assessment is key to scaling falls prevention programs.

Step 3: Intervene Based on Assessment Results

The intervention step is personalized. For low-risk patients (normal results on all tests), provide counselling on exercise, medication review, and home safety. For moderate-risk patients (some test results abnormal), offer structured exercise programs such as tai chi or strength training, address vision and hearing problems, and optimize medications. For high-risk patients (multiple abnormal results or prior falls), consider physical therapy referral, home safety assessment, and medical investigation for underlying conditions causing imbalance.

Interventions must be evidence-based. Exercise is the cornerstone: programs combining strength, balance, and flexibility training reduce fall risk by 20-30%. Vision screening and correction prevent 23% of falls. Medication optimization, particularly reducing central nervous system depressants, prevents another 22%. Home safety modifications prevent falls in 25% of cases when implemented in high-risk individuals. Combining interventions multiplies their effect.

Document intervention plans clearly. Patients should understand what was found, why it matters, and what they should do next. Consider written summaries, structured follow-up appointments, and communication with other care providers to ensure coordinated action.

blocks.heading: Implementing STEADI in Your Facility: 5 Key Steps

steps.heading: Establish baseline and set goals
steps.description: Audit your current fall rate and fall-related injuries. Set a target for risk-stratified screening coverage (e.g., 80% of patients 65+ within 12 months). Identify champions: a falls prevention lead, clinical staff, and administrative support.

steps.heading: Train staff on the 3 Key Questions and functional tests
steps.description: Deliver 15-30 minute training sessions covering screening workflow, test administration, and documentation. Use video demonstrations and practice sessions. Credentials (certificates) reinforce accountability. Include scenarios on handling falls and escalation pathways.

steps.heading: Integrate screening into routine visits
steps.description: Embed the 3 Key Questions into annual wellness visits, preventive care checks, and disease management appointments. Train front-desk staff to prompt clinical staff when patients are due for screening. Use electronic health record (EHR) alerts and flags.

steps.heading: Perform assessments using Able Assess or manual protocols
steps.description: For organizations using Able Assess, train staff on device setup, test administration via the app, and result interpretation. For manual testing, ensure staff understand proper positioning, timing, and safety practices. Store results securely and integrate with EHR systems.

steps.heading: Plan and execute interventions with tracking
steps.description: Create intervention pathways for low-, moderate-, and high-risk groups. Document plans in the EHR. Schedule follow-up appointments. Track engagement with interventions (e.g., exercise program attendance, medication changes). Measure outcomes at 6 and 12 months.

blocks.body:
Digital Implementation With Able Assess

Able Assess accelerates STEADI implementation by automating functional testing and result interpretation. The platform guides staff through the 4 core tests (grip strength, 30-second chair stand, Timed Up and Go, and 4-stage balance), records results automatically, and generates risk stratification instantly. This reduces testing time from 15 minutes to 5-8 minutes and eliminates manual calculation errors.

Integration with your EHR or care management system ensures results sync automatically, eliminating re-entry and creating a longitudinal record of fall risk trends. Able Assess supports non-clinical staff administration, meaning screening programs can expand beyond physiotherapists and nurses. Training takes under 15 minutes, and built-in coaching within the app reinforces proper technique.

The digital record also enables population-level monitoring. Organizations can view fall risk distributions across patient cohorts, track screening completion rates, and measure intervention uptake and outcomes. This data supports continuous improvement and demonstrates program impact to leadership and funders.

Measuring STEADI Outcomes and Demonstrating Impact

Effective implementation requires outcome measurement. Track these metrics: screening completion rate (percentage of eligible patients screened), risk distribution (percentage classified as low, moderate, high), intervention uptake (percentage offered and accepting interventions), fall incidence before and after (number of falls per 1,000 patient-months), and fall-related injuries (hospitalizations, fractures, head injuries).

Establish a baseline period (6-12 months pre-implementation) to measure existing fall rates and injury patterns. Then implement STEADI progressively across your organization. After 6-12 months, compare fall incidence, injury severity, and healthcare costs. Most studies report 15-30% reductions in fall incidence and 20-40% reductions in fall-related injuries within 12 months of full STEADI implementation.

Report outcomes regularly to clinical teams and leadership. Highlight both safety and economic benefits: falls prevention is cost-effective, with every dollar spent on structured prevention programs yielding 3-5 dollars in healthcare savings. Use these data to sustain program momentum, secure funding, and expand to other facilities within your organization.

blocks.heading: What the Evidence Says About STEADI

stats.stat: STEADI-based interventions reduce fall incidence by 17-24%
Exercise programs integrated with STEADI screening reduce fall risk by 20-30%
1 in 4 adults aged 65+ falls annually; direct medical costs reached $50 billion in 2015, projected to reach $101 billion by 2030
stats.source: CDC National Center for Injury Prevention and Control, 2015-2024

blocks.body:
Common Implementation Challenges and Solutions

Challenge: Staff perceive screening as time-consuming. Solution: Emphasize that the 3 Key Questions take 2-3 minutes, and functional assessment takes 5-15 minutes. Start with high-volume clinics where volume justifies the time investment. Demonstrate quick wins: high-risk patients who avoid serious falls justify the program retrospectively.

Challenge: Insufficient training or knowledge of falls prevention among staff. Solution: Develop a structured training curriculum with role-specific modules (clinical staff, support staff, administrative). Use video demonstrations, competency assessments, and refresher sessions. Designate a falls prevention champion to provide ongoing support and troubleshooting.

Challenge: Difficulty identifying and securing funding for programs. Solution: Model the economic case: falls prevention yields 3-5 dollars of healthcare savings per dollar spent. Partner with quality improvement teams, patient safety initiatives, and insurance providers. Seek grants from public health agencies and falls prevention charities.

items.question: Do I need to implement STEADI across my entire organization at once?
items.answer: No. Start with one high-volume clinic or care unit. Demonstrate success, refine workflows, build staff confidence, then expand. Pilot programs lasting 3-6 months provide evidence for rolling out organization-wide.

items.question: What if a patient refuses testing or intervention?
items.answer: Document the refusal clearly. Provide education on fall risks and benefits of screening. Offer alternative interventions or simpler assessments. Respect autonomy while ensuring the patient understands the risks. Revisit at future visits.

items.question: How do I track outcomes when my organization uses multiple EHR systems?
items.answer: Start with a simple registry using spreadsheets or a dedicated falls prevention database. Record patient demographics, screening results, interventions, and outcomes. Once you've demonstrated value, invest in EHR integration or a platform like Able Assess that bridges systems.

items.question: Can non-clinical staff administer the functional tests reliably?
items.answer: Yes. A systematic review in the Journal of the American Geriatrics Society confirmed that trained health workers, care coordinators, and community health workers achieve equivalent reliability to clinical physiotherapists with proper training and quality assurance.

items.question: How often should I re-screen patients who are not at high risk?
items.answer: Re-screen annually as part of preventive care. Re-screen immediately if a patient reports a fall, new mobility problems, or medication changes. High-risk patients should be re-screened every 3-6 months or after significant life events.

blocks.heading: Ready to implement STEADI in your organization?
blocks.bodyText: Able Care can help you establish screening programs, train staff, and measure outcomes.
